prompt: Which role is typically accountable for overall TPRM program governance?
choices: TPRM program owner defining policy, standards, and oversight cadence
Any requester who submits a vendor onboarding form
Only procurement interns
No defined role is required
explanation: A designated program owner ensures consistent policy execution and accountability across vendor lifecycles.

prompt: What is the best role separation in TPRM assessment workflows?
choices: Assessor also self-approves all findings
Distinct roles for assessor, control owner, and approver
Only one role for all activities
Approvals delegated to unrelated users
explanation: Segregation of duties reduces bias and strengthens review integrity.

prompt: Which TPRM role should own remediation execution for vendor issues?
choices: Vendor manager or assigned issue owner with due-date accountability
Any read-only user
Portal guest account
System diagnostics user
explanation: Named ownership with due dates ensures issues move from identification to closure.

prompt: How should access be granted for TPRM reporting consumers?
choices: Grant full admin rights to all viewers
Use least-privilege role groups aligned to data sensitivity and reporting needs
Share raw tables without ACL controls
Disable role-based access for dashboards
explanation: Least-privilege access protects vendor risk data while enabling appropriate visibility.

prompt: Which sign of role-model maturity is most meaningful in TPRM?
choices: Frequent reassignment of issue ownership
Clear RACI coverage with minimal approval bottlenecks
Untracked exceptions approved by email
No distinction between assessors and approvers
explanation: A clear RACI with efficient handoffs indicates sustainable governance and execution.

prompt: Which role is primarily responsible for VR policy and operational governance?
choices: Vulnerability response manager or program owner
Any business user with portal access
Only CMDB data steward
No ownership role is needed
explanation: Program ownership is needed to maintain lifecycle standards, SLAs, and remediation accountability.

prompt: What role should approve high-impact vulnerability exceptions?
choices: Scanner integration user
Designated risk approver with security governance authority
Any technician assigned to the item
Service desk trainee
explanation: Exception approval for high risk requires formal authority and risk accountability.

prompt: Which role should typically own remediation tasks in VR?
choices: Application or infrastructure owner responsible for affected assets
Only platform administrators
Unassigned queue with no owner
Security auditors only
explanation: Asset and application owners are best positioned to execute and validate remediation changes.

prompt: How should read access be handled for vulnerability data with sensitive findings?
choices: Open to all authenticated users
Role-based access with constraints for sensitive CI and exploit details
Shared through public reports
No ACLs needed when dashboards exist
explanation: Sensitive vulnerability details require controlled visibility based on operational need.

prompt: What indicates healthy VR role alignment?
choices: Remediation, approval, and review duties are clearly separated with low reassignment churn
All responsibilities assigned to one responder
No role ownership captured on records
Frequent overdue items with unknown owners
explanation: Clear ownership and segregation improve throughput, auditability, and risk control.

prompt: Which role is typically accountable for CSM process governance?
choices: CSM process owner defining case policies and service standards
Any customer contact at random
Only procurement manager
No formal role ownership
explanation: A process owner ensures consistent operational standards across case lifecycle workflows.

prompt: What role should own entitlement policy decisions in CSM?
choices: Contract/service owner with governance authority
Any case agent editing the case
Guest user from portal
Catalog item approver only
explanation: Entitlement policy ownership should map to contractual and service accountability.

prompt: How should escalated CSM cases be role-assigned?
choices: Leave in original queue without ownership change
Route to designated escalation roles with decision authority
Assign to inactive users
Close automatically when escalated
explanation: Escalation roles should have the authority and context to resolve high-impact customer issues quickly.

prompt: What access model is best for CSM account data?
choices: Broad read/write access for all case agents
Role and account-based access controls aligned to assigned customer portfolios
Public dashboards with customer details
No differentiation between internal and external users
explanation: Account-scoped access reduces confidentiality risk while enabling effective support operations.

prompt: Which symptom suggests CSM role design needs improvement?
choices: Frequent manual reassignment due to unclear ownership boundaries
Stable ownership and low escalation delay
Consistent entitlement enforcement
Low backlog for high-priority cases
explanation: Repeated reassignment is a common indicator of role ambiguity and poor process routing.

prompt: In FSM mobile, what is the key value of offline capability?
choices: Technicians can complete assigned work and capture updates without network connectivity
It disables all task forms on mobile
It only works for dispatchers in office
It prevents attachment capture
explanation: Offline mode enables uninterrupted field execution in low-connectivity environments.

prompt: What should be configured first for reliable FSM mobile offline operations?
choices: The company newsletter feed
Data synchronization rules for required records and task updates
Desktop browser cache settings
Only UI themes and icons
explanation: Sync scope determines what data is available and what updates can be safely queued offline.

prompt: How should conflict handling work when offline updates sync back online?
choices: Discard all offline edits automatically
Apply conflict resolution rules and surface exceptions for review
Overwrite master data without controls
Force technician logout permanently
explanation: Controlled conflict handling preserves data integrity when concurrent edits occur.

prompt: Which offline KPI is most useful for FSM support teams?
choices: Percentage of successful sync retries after reconnection
Number of desktop bookmarks
Count of inactive user photos
Average email size
explanation: Sync retry success reflects offline reliability and recovery quality in the field.

prompt: What administrative control improves security for FSM offline data?
choices: Disable device authentication
Enforce mobile security policies, encryption, and role-based data scope
Store all field data in public folders
Allow shared credentials across technicians
explanation: Offline data must remain protected through strong device and access controls.

prompt: In HRSD, what is the primary role of the service catalog for employees?
choices: To provide a structured entry point for HR services and requests
To replace HR case management entirely
To hide available services from employees
To manage only IT incidents
explanation: The HR service catalog standardizes request intake and improves employee self-service.

prompt: Which configuration best supports targeted HR services by population?
choices: Publish all items to everyone
Use HR criteria to control catalog item visibility and eligibility
Hide all items by default with no criteria
Use assignment groups only for visibility
explanation: HR criteria ensure employees see relevant services based on role, location, and policy context.

prompt: How should HR catalog items be designed for fulfillment efficiency?
choices: Require many free-text fields for all requests
Use guided forms with necessary data and mapped fulfillment workflows
Avoid using templates
Create one generic item for every HR need
explanation: Guided, data-complete requests reduce triage effort and improve fulfillment accuracy.

prompt: Which metric best evaluates HR catalog effectiveness?
choices: Number of catalog categories created
Request completion rate without manual rework
Count of inactive users
Average browser session duration
explanation: High completion without rework indicates clear forms and strong workflow alignment.

prompt: What governance practice keeps the HR service catalog healthy over time?
choices: Leave outdated items active indefinitely
Perform periodic catalog reviews for usage, policy alignment, and content quality
Disable all employee feedback
Allow duplicate items for each team
explanation: Regular governance prevents stale content and preserves service relevance and compliance.
